instruction: Audit each MCQ. Correct answer_index + clinical hinge. Set flip if changing answer.
system:
You are auditing KSA SDLE / Prometric-style dental MCQs for a study app.
Rules:
- Community/PDF ✅ marks are often wrong — decide by clinical board standard, not the mark.
- Output ONLY a valid JSON array (no markdown fences).
- Each object MUST have:
  id, answer_index (0-3), confidence (high|med|low),
  hinge (clinical why, 1-3 sentences, English),
  flip (boolean: true if answer_index differs from answer_index_in),
  department (operative|fixed|rpd|endo|perio|oms|ortho_pedo|ethics|mixed)
- If image-only / impossible: answer_index null, usable false, hinge explains.
- operative = direct restorations/preps/materials technique; fixed/rpd = prostho; do not dump all into restorative.
- Do not invent textbook page numbers.

items.q: What is the cause of gross (ismuth )amalgam fracture
items.explanation_in: Gross or isthmus fracture of amalgam is most often from inadequate preparation: insufficient depth/width, sharp internal line angles, or a thin weak isthmus that concentrates stress under load. High occlusal force can precipitate failure but usually acts on an already underprepared or overextended restoration. Etched porcelain and GI/diet options are not causes of amalgam bulk fracture.

items.q: A newborn live in non-fluoridated community, what is the amount of fluoride supplement he should take
options: gingival swelling buccal to the tooth -2
items.explanation_in: From birth to 6 months, fluoride supplement dose is 0 mg/day even in nonfluoridated areas; supplementation tables start only after 6 months of age based on water F level.

items.q: years old male Pt with history of MI 1 year ago and ( Hepatitis B treated 4 years ago ) his chief complain Pain on #43 and the tooth is necrotic you planned to do RCT. What medication you will give to the Pt
items.explanation_in: MI one year prior (stable) and resolved hepatitis B do not mandate prolonged antibiotics for routine RCT; short-course NSAID analgesia (naproxen ~5 days) is appropriate for post-endo pain control.

items.q: Pt with reverse smoking habit came with white lesion on the palate. What is the nature of the lesion
items.explanation_in: Reverse smoking produces thermal/chemical irritation of the palate yielding a reactive keratotic/stomatitis-type white change, not primarily a malignant or purely ‘benign neoplasm’ label on first presentation.

items.q: What is the appropriate way to diagnose fibromyalgia
items.explanation_in: Fibromyalgia has no single pathognomonic lab or biopsy; diagnosis is clinical and by exclusion of other rheumatic, neurologic, and endocrine causes of widespread pain.

items.q: years old female Pt with history of fibromyalgia rheumatica complaining of pain on the right side of her face and loss of appetite and pain when she closes her mouth. What is your diagnosis? ( They
options: ) never felt a pain like this before
items.explanation_in: New severe unilateral facial/jaw pain with systemic features (anorexia) in a patient with polymyalgia rheumatica spectrum raises giant cell arteritis until proven otherwise—urgent, not simple myalgia.

items.q: Cause of effectiveness of Chlorhexidine
items.explanation_in: Chlorhexidine’s clinical effectiveness rests largely on substantivity—binding to oral surfaces and releasing over hours—rather than tissue dissolution or nonspecific cytotoxicity.

items.q: Picture of External root resorption. What is the type of resorption
items.explanation_in: Among external resorption types, surface (repair) resorption is the shallow, self-limiting cemental loss often shown as minimal external contour change; inflammatory and replacement types have more aggressive radiographic patterns.

items.q: Pedo pt came with her mother. he was playing with the Dental instruments and always lose focus and attention. What is your diagnosis
options: Soak them in non ionizing solution
items.explanation_in: A child who cannot sustain attention, is constantly handling instruments, and loses focus fits attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder behaviorally among the coherent options given.

items.q: After injecting lidocaine 2%, patient feels palpitation
items.explanation_in: Immediate palpitations after lidocaine (especially with epinephrine) most often mean inadvertent intravascular injection delivering a catecholamine bolus, not full systemic toxicity alone.

items.q: Patient wearing upper complete and lower RPD has attrition on lower anterior and epulis fissuratum when removing lower RPD , what will you see when removing upper complete
options: Soak them in non ionizing solution
items.explanation_in: Combination syndrome pattern (upper CD + lower anterior teeth/RPD) yields flabby anterior maxillary ridge with epulis fissuratum and lower anterior overeruption/attrition when the upper prosthesis is removed.

items.q: Submandibular fluctuant swelling with firm mass. Palpable. Radiographic showing opaque mass that is palpable and painful
items.explanation_in: A painful submandibular swelling with a firm palpable mass and a radiopaque ductal body is sialolithiasis (stone), not non-inflammatory sialadenosis or a simple duct cyst.

items.q: Patient to do implant of 11 , what is essential
items.explanation_in: For a single anterior implant (#11), essential planning is prosthetically driven: diagnostic casts/wax-up and a surgical template so 3D position matches the final crown. Closed-tray impression is a transfer technique after placement, not the planning essential. Non-engaging abutments relate to multi-unit passivity, not single-tooth esthetic planning. GI/diet investigation is not required for implant site prosthetics.

items.q: in lower premolar with pain while releasing from occlusion
items.explanation_in: Pain on release of biting force (rebound pain) is the classic clinical hinge for a cracked tooth: dentinal flexure closes on load and opens on release, stimulating pulp. Vertical root fracture more often shows deep isolated probing and J-shaped bone loss, typically after endo/post placement—not isolated pain on release. Etched porcelain and GI/diet workup do not explain this bite-release pattern.

items.q: pedo patient with primary molar indicated for extraction which of these are defi nitive diagnosis for extraction
options: gingival swelling buccal to the tooth -2
items.explanation_in: Buccal gingival swelling over a primary molar indicates abscess/acute infection and is a stronger definitive indication for extraction than isolated percussion pain alone.

items.q: tooth #21 with substandard RCT with cast post and core well crowned ( it was written like this in the )exam
items.explanation_in: Substandard RCT under a well-fitting cast post–core and crown makes orthograde retreatment highly destructive or impractical (post removal risks root fracture). Apical surgery with root-end resection, preparation, and retrofill is the usual definitive path to seal the apex. Surgery without retrofill leaves an inadequate seal; vague numbered retreatment options that omit a proper retrofill miss the critical apical seal step.

items.q: pt referred from her psychiatrist because of submandibular swelling which of these may cause it
options: Soak them in non ionizing solution
items.explanation_in: Among psychiatric contexts, eating disorders (bulimia/purging) are classically linked to bilateral salivary gland enlargement/swelling; schizophrenia and bipolar are not primary causes of submandibular swellings.

items.q: Uncontrolled DM , 2 days ago he has swelling left side with sever trismus but breath normally , what is most appropriate? management
options: Soak them in non ionizing solution
Immediately admit hospital and take CT
items.explanation_in: Uncontrolled diabetes with acute facial swelling and severe trismus risks deep space infection and airway compromise; immediate hospital admission with CT and surgical/medical management is required.

items.q: Pt is well controlled diabetic(takes metformin) HBA1c was 5.6 last year last few months he was diagnosed with arthritis and corticosteroid and methotrexate were prescribed for him what could happen if he continue using? these medications
options: Soak them in non ionizing solution
items.explanation_in: Systemic corticosteroids raise blood glucose and can worsen glycemic control; continued steroid use in a diabetic predictably increases HbA1c despite prior good control on metformin.

items.q: When a patient with expired insurance coverage request you to continue treatment and accept payment once , their insuracnce renews before , what course of action should you take: Proceed with the treatment and accept payment when the insurance is
options: Provide treatment only if patient agrees to pay out of pocket
items.explanation_in: With expired coverage, you may treat if the patient agrees to private/out-of-pocket payment; you should not bill as if insurance will retroactively cover or delay care on a false insurance promise alone.

items.q: years old female Pt came to replace non restorable tooth with implant after radiographic examination there was multiple punched out radiolucency that are different in size what is your diagnosis
items.explanation_in: Multiple punched-out radiolucencies of varying size in an adult (often skull/jaws) are classic for multiple myeloma (or sometimes metastases). Gorlin (nevoid basal cell carcinoma) syndrome features multiple odontogenic keratocysts, usually in younger patients, not innumerable punched-out skull lesions. These findings are not normal aging. GI/diet workup does not explain multifocal punched-out bone lesions before implant planning.

items.q: Pt came with very deep caries you suspect that it is going to need crown lengthening what is the appropriate sequence
items.explanation_in: For deep caries possibly needing crown lengthening: excavate first to judge restorability and pulp status; obtain bitewings to assess remaining tooth structure, biologic width, and ferrule needs; complete RCT if indicated; then perform surgical crown lengthening before definitive buildup/crown. Doing RCT before radiographic assessment of remaining structure, or lengthening before knowing restorability, can lead to non-restorable roots or unnecessary surgery.

items.q: Which one of the following had highest ph
items.explanation_in: Sodium hypochlorite irrigant is strongly alkaline (pH ~11–12), higher than chlorhexidine (~5.5–7), normal saline (~7), or phosphoric acid (very low pH).

items.q: most inorganic component in dentin
items.explanation_in: The predominant inorganic component of dentin is hydroxyapatite (calcium phosphate mineral); collagen types are the organic matrix, not inorganic.

items.q: patient with diffuse erythematous gingiva, and gray circle ulcers in mucosa and tongue, what should you give
items.explanation_in: Diffuse erythematous gingivitis with oral mucosal ulcers in a viral primary herpetic pattern is managed with antivirals plus supportive care; antibiotics alone do not treat HSV and steroids can worsen viral disease.

items.q: What is the antagonist of midazolam
items.explanation_in: Flumazenil is the competitive benzodiazepine receptor antagonist used to reverse midazolam sedation or overdose.

items.q: Why flowable better than packable in PRR
items.explanation_in: In preventive resin restorations, flowable composite wets and fills narrow pits/fissures better because of lower viscosity and lower filler load than packable materials.

items.q: pt 5 years old with negative behavior and multiple occlusal caries
items.explanation_in: In a 5-year-old with negative behavior and multiple occlusal lesions, professionally applied fluoride as a varnish wiped/painted on is preferred: short chair time, no trays, low gag risk, and lower systemic ingestion risk than gel. Tray-delivered fluoride gel needs longer retention and cooperation and raises ingestion risk in uncooperative kids. “Wipe with gel” still uses a less sticky, higher-ingestion-risk vehicle than varnish.

items.q: the other name for organic occlusion
items.explanation_in: In classic occlusion teaching, “organic occlusion” means mutually protected occlusion: posteriors take load in MIP and protect anteriors, while anterior guidance discludes posteriors in excursion. Centric and eccentric describe jaw positions/movements, not the protective scheme. Group function is an alternative lateral scheme (shared posterior contact in working excursion), not the synonym for organic/mutually protected occlusion.

items.q: Paul Bunnell positive
items.explanation_in: A positive Paul–Bunnell (heterophile antibody) test supports infectious mononucleosis from EBV; it is not used to diagnose HSV, HPV, or SLE.
